transcript.transcript: Okay. Hi, my name is Aleta Sales Pottenger, and I'm doing an interview today with Kaye Andrewski about her life experience with dance in the Treasure Valley. Um, today is September 5th, 2025. And, Kay, could you give me the spelling of your name as you would like to be remembered? Okay, my first name is Kaye and it's spelled k a y e. Sorry I'm so croaky today. Oh. You're perfect. My middle name is Nora N.o.r.a. And my last name is Andrijeski. A n d r i j e s k i. My maiden name was Mitchell. M I T C H E L L. Perfect. That's exactly what we needed. And do I have your permission to record this interview? Of course. Fantastic. And today we're going to learn about your life experiences, your reflections, and the times you've lived through. Does that sound like fun? Well, we can try. My memory is not all that good anymore. Well, I think your memory is fantastic. First, I want to say thank you so much for giving this time to me today. And this will, uh, allow us to reflect and share your memories and experiences about your life and contributions. Contributions to dance. So I'm really excited that you agreed to do this. Uh, so my first question to you is, when and where were you born? I was born in Nampa, Idaho, December 29th, 1934. Excellent. And what hospital were you born in? Mercy hospital in Napa. Very good. And, um, the original building sat vacant for a long time. I've done a little research, and then it was demolished and it was rebuilt. So you were. Yeah. Interestingly, uh, the first hospital was built in 1919, and I. I'm guessing that probably was the hospital that you were born in. I would guess so, yes. I think I would have known if they'd torn it down and built a new one. Yes. Yes, indeed. In fact, I do remember when they did that. It was after I was born. Oh. Very good. Excellent. Quite a bit. And, um, I'm going to ask you a few questions about your childhood. Um. Okay. Your parents, were they from Idaho? Uh, no. My mother was born in Illinois. And my father. You know what? I've forgotten where he was born. That's okay. Don't remember. But he wasn't born in Idaho. So you're a first generation idahoan? I guess so, yeah. Yeah. And you had children? Six, as I recall. And they were all born in Idaho. They were all born. Oh, no, that's not right. The second one, Julie, was born in the hospital at, uh. Um. Oh. Can't think of the name of the air base, um, Fort Lewis, Washington. Okay, so our second child was born there because my husband had been called up in, in a crisis, and we were there for about a year. Yeah, yeah. And I know we've talked about your dad before. What was his name? George Mitchell. And he owned a box factory in Nampa. Mhm. And it was called the Idaho Box Factory. Is that right? Yes. That's right. Mhm. One of my things that I love, that you've told me before is uh, the fact that you love the smell of fresh cut wood. Yes. And I still do. Except the sad part is I no longer have a sense of smell. That is sad. I'm sorry to hear that. Sad. I love fragrances and I can't enjoy that anymore. So enjoy it while you can. That's good advice. I love that. Okay. I love that. And you've been married to Harlan for how many years? Um, 66 this August 22nd. Wow. That's that's an amazing, amazing amount of time. Congratulations. That's a long time to live with one person. It is, it is. But what a testimony. I'm so proud of you. And thank. You. Tell me about your sister. My sister. My sister was two and a half years older. She was beautiful. She had naturally curly black hair. And she was with Miss Nappa one year. And she and I both took dance classes from a lady named Mae Van De Steeg in Nampa, Idaho. And both of us went on to teach dance, tap and ballet and some acrobatics. Um, my sister married and had seven children. She had to outdo me by one. And. And, uh, she was beautiful and talented. And I miss her. Yes. I'm sorry for your loss. Um, I want you to tell me about the fact that you and your sister taught dance in Marsing, Idaho. Yes, that's correct. And we did it for a number of years. I'm not sure how many. Right. And there was a club over there. Can you tell me about the ladies club? It was. Oh, no, I can't really tell you much about that. There's a clubhouse when you drive into Marsing, you drive through the main part of town, and there's a one big major curve, and there's a little white building. Or there used to be a little white building that was called the Wohelo Club, uh, for ladies in Marsing. They met there, and then they allowed us to teach our dance classes there on Saturdays. Right. And you girls were quite young when you started teaching? Yes. In fact, I think Joan was 14 and I was 12 because she was just barely old enough to drive us there. Right, right. And I love the fact that you told me that you played the piano for each other's classes. Yes we did. Mhm. That must have been kind of sad, but I guess it worked. I'm sure it worked. I'm sure it worked. Yeah. That's fantastic. So, uh, tell me what year you graduated from Nampa High School? 1953. And did you study dance from the time you were a child? All through high school? Um, yeah, I guess we did. Yeah. Mhm. So, uh, you also told me about a woman that you had met, um, uh, Drid Williams. Drid d r i d. Exactly. Mhm. Yes. And did you study with her too? Yes I did. Oh I went to school uh, at Marylhurst in Portland outside of Portland, and she was a teacher in Portland. And I would go in every week for classes. Mhm. And I did a little research on her. She is a or was a very famous woman. Oh is she really. Yes. Yes. I guess I didn't know that. I'd be interested in seeing your research. Okay. So she she had a definite influence on your life? Yes. She did. Uh huh. Yeah. And, um, post high school. Can you tell me where you went to school? Yeah. Yeah. You know, one of the things I remember us talking about one time was, um. How important faith has been to you in your life? What had been. Faith? your faith, how important it was. Well, I I'm going to mention that our lives intersected when I was in junior high school. And a fellow dance friend of yours from Nampa, Pat Harris, um, bought a studio in Boise. Yes. And you were my ballet teacher. And I loved that. And I love you. Oh, and I love you too. I still think fondly back about how graceful and, um. I remember you telling me that your mother loved the arts. She did? Yes. She was. She was a teacher. Right. And she continued teaching. Yes. Yes. Absolutely. And I know you had something tragic happen in your family, um, with your dad. Well, he died quite young. He was only 46 when he died. Absolutely. Very young. And I'm sorry for your loss. You've told me before that it could have been prevented in today's world. Yes. He had very high blood pressure, and they hadn't learned to treat that yet for him. Right, right. And I think you told me he was on a trip. Um. I don't know about that. Okay. Okay. But they did take a train for him to have extended treatment, which did not work. Because she was a year behind me at, uh, at, uh, the high school. And, of course, she was taking class from Mrs. Van to. Exactly, exactly. And how many years did you teach ballet for Pat Harris? Oh, gosh. You know, I don't even know. Well, I know it was a long time. Many, many years. Very much so. That's nice. So. Yes. Yeah. You touched so many lives in such a a wonderful way. metadata.title: Introduction and Permissions
metadata.synopsis: The interviewer, Aleta Sales Pottenger, introduces herself and the purpose of the interview with Kaye Andrijeski, focusing on Kaye's experiences with dance in the Treasure Valley. Kaye provides the spelling of her name and gives permission for the interview to be recorded. The tone is friendly and sets the stage for a reflective conversation about Kaye's life and contributions to dance.
metadata.keywords: Kaye Andrijeski, Aleta Sales Pottenger, Treasure Valley

lines.line: # Birth and Early Childhood

> Kay shares details about her birth, including the date, location, and hospital in Nampa, Idaho. The interviewer provides historical context about the hospital, and Kay confirms her connection to the original building. The conversation establishes Kay's roots in Idaho and sets up further discussion about her family background.

metadata.title: Birth and Early Childhood
metadata.synopsis: Kay shares details about her birth, including the date, location, and hospital in Nampa, Idaho. The interviewer provides historical context about the hospital, and Kay confirms her connection to the original building. The conversation establishes Kay's roots in Idaho and sets up further discussion about her family background.

lines.line: # Family Background and Siblings

> Kay discusses her family, mentioning her six children and clarifying that one was born in Washington due to her husband's military service. She shares information about her parents, including her father's business, the Idaho Box Factory, and her love for the smell of fresh cut wood. The chapter also touches on her long marriage to Harlan and introduces her sister, Joan, highlighting their close relationship and shared involvement in dance.

lines.line: # Early Dance Education and Teaching

> Kay describes her sister Joan's beauty and talent, their shared dance education under Mae Van De Steeg, and their early experiences teaching dance, tap, ballet, and acrobatics in Marsing, Idaho. She recalls the Wohelo Club, where they taught classes as teenagers, and their collaborative approach, including playing piano for each other's classes.

metadata.title: Early Dance Education and Teaching

She recalls the Wohelo Club, where they taught classes as teenagers, and their collaborative approach, including playing piano for each other's classes.

lines.line: # High School and Further Dance Studies

> Kay reveals she graduated from Nampa High School in 1953 and continued studying dance throughout her youth. She discusses her influential teacher, Dread Williams, whom she studied with in Portland, and learns more about Dread's background as an anthropologist, author, and choreographer. The chapter highlights the impact of dedicated mentors on Kay's development as a dancer.

metadata.title: High School and Further Dance Studies

She discusses her influential teacher, Dread Williams, whom she studied with in Portland, and learns more about Dread's background as an anthropologist, author, and choreographer. The chapter highlights the impact of dedicated mentors on Kay's development as a dancer.

lines.line: # College, Work, and Life in Other Cities

> Kay outlines her educational journey, starting with Saint Paul's in Nampa, then Nampa Junior High and High School, followed by Marylhurst College in Oregon. She reflects on her decision to leave college and move to San Francisco, where she worked and enjoyed city life, and later lived in New York City, working in the public library and continuing her dance studies. These experiences broadened her perspective and enriched her life beyond Idaho.

lines.line: # Sister’s Path and Faith

> Kay explains that her sister attended college but did not graduate, choosing instead to marry and start a family. The conversation shifts to the importance of faith in Kay's life, her Catholic upbringing, and her efforts to raise her children in the faith, acknowledging that not all of them remained Catholic. Kay reflects on the personal nature of religious belief and its enduring significance for her.

The chapter emphasizes the role of faith and tradition in shaping her family life.

Kay expresses fondness for her students and reflects on the grace and poise developed through dance training, highlighting the lasting impact of her teaching.

lines.line: # Mother’s Influence and Father’s Death

> Kay describes her mother's passion for the arts and her career as a teacher in Nampa, particularly at Kenwood Grade School. She shares the tragic story of her father's early death from untreated high blood pressure and reflects on how medical advances might have prevented it. After her father's passing, her mother returned to teaching, demonstrating resilience and dedication to her family.

lines.line: # Teaching Ballet for Pat Harris

> Kay recounts her long-standing relationship with Pat Harris, whom she knew from high school and dance classes with Mrs. Van De Steeg. She discusses her years teaching ballet for Pat Harris, the positive impact on generations of students, and the supportive environment that allowed her to focus on teaching while raising her own children. Kay acknowledges Pat Harris's significant role in fostering dance education in the community.

Kay recalls teaching for Lloyd and the shared experiences of dance teachers, including joint performances and the Boise Valley Dance Teachers Association, which promoted unity and professional development among local instructors.

The chapter highlights the sense of community and shared purpose among dance educators.

lines.line: # Grandparents and Heritage

> Kay shares memories of her grandmother Nora Morgan Whissel and her family's heritage, including Irish, German, and English roots. She recalls visiting relatives in Illinois and describes her grandparents' physical characteristics, noting the generational changes in height. The discussion provides insight into Kay's ancestry and the diverse cultural influences in her family.

lines.line: # Mrs. Van De Steeg and Dance Teaching Traditions

> Kay talks about her dance teacher Mrs. Van De Steeg, who settled in Nampa after marrying a local attorney. She describes Mrs. Van De Steeg's background in traveling performance, her distinctive teaching style using a stick and wearing heels, and the traditional methods of dance instruction in that era. Kay contrasts these practices with her own approach to teaching dance.

lines.line: # Closing Reflections

> The interview concludes with Kay expressing satisfaction with the topics covered and appreciation for the opportunity to document her life story. The interviewer thanks Kay for her time and contributions to dance in the Treasure Valley, offering to record additional sessions if Kay wishes to add more memories in the future. The conversation ends on a warm and grateful note.

metadata.title: Closing Reflections
metadata.synopsis: The interview concludes with Kay expressing satisfaction with the topics covered and appreciation for the opportunity to document her life story. The interviewer thanks Kay for her time and contributions to dance in the Treasure Valley, offering to record additional sessions if Kay wishes to add more memories in the future. The conversation ends on a warm and grateful note.

children.text: # Introduction and Permissions

> The interviewer, Aleta Sales Pottenger, introduces herself and the purpose of the interview with Kaye Andrijeski, focusing on Kaye's experiences with dance in the Treasure Valley. Kaye provides the spelling of her name and gives permission for the interview to be recorded. The tone is friendly and sets the stage for a reflective conversation about Kaye's life and contributions to dance.
**Kaye Andrijeski, Aleta Sales Pottenger, Treasure Valley**
# Birth and Early Childhood

> Kay shares details about her birth, including the date, location, and hospital in Nampa, Idaho. The interviewer provides historical context about the hospital, and Kay confirms her connection to the original building. The conversation establishes Kay's roots in Idaho and sets up further discussion about her family background.
# Family Background and Siblings

> Kay discusses her family, mentioning her six children and clarifying that one was born in Washington due to her husband's military service. She shares information about her parents, including her father's business, the Idaho Box Factory, and her love for the smell of fresh cut wood. The chapter also touches on her long marriage to Harlan and introduces her sister, Joan, highlighting their close relationship and shared involvement in dance.
# Early Dance Education and Teaching

> Kay describes her sister Joan's beauty and talent, their shared dance education under Mae Van De Steeg, and their early experiences teaching dance, tap, ballet, and acrobatics in Marsing, Idaho. She recalls the Wohelo Club, where they taught classes as teenagers, and their collaborative approach, including playing piano for each other's classes.
# High School and Further Dance Studies

> Kay reveals she graduated from Nampa High School in 1953 and continued studying dance throughout her youth. She discusses her influential teacher, Dread Williams, whom she studied with in Portland, and learns more about Dread's background as an anthropologist, author, and choreographer. The chapter highlights the impact of dedicated mentors on Kay's development as a dancer.
# College, Work, and Life in Other Cities

> Kay outlines her educational journey, starting with Saint Paul's in Nampa, then Nampa Junior High and High School, followed by Marylhurst College in Oregon. She reflects on her decision to leave college and move to San Francisco, where she worked and enjoyed city life, and later lived in New York City, working in the public library and continuing her dance studies. These experiences broadened her perspective and enriched her life beyond Idaho.
# Sister’s Path and Faith

> Kay explains that her sister attended college but did not graduate, choosing instead to marry and start a family. The conversation shifts to the importance of faith in Kay's life, her Catholic upbringing, and her efforts to raise her children in the faith, acknowledging that not all of them remained Catholic. Kay reflects on the personal nature of religious belief and its enduring significance for her.
# Marriage, Family, and Catholic Roots

> Kay confirms her marriage in Saint Paul's Catholic Church in Nampa and discusses her parochial school education. She shares details about her college years and reiterates the importance of her Catholic roots, both in her upbringing and in raising her children. The chapter emphasizes the role of faith and tradition in shaping her family life.
# Other Studies and Intersection with Interviewer

> Kay talks about her studies in piano and acrobatics alongside dance. The interviewer shares a personal connection, recalling Kay as her ballet teacher at Pat Harris's studio in Boise. Kay expresses fondness for her students and reflects on the grace and poise developed through dance training, highlighting the lasting impact of her teaching.
# Boise Music Week and Arts Contributions

> Kay and her husband Harland's involvement in Boise Music Week is discussed, including Harland's role as president. Their contributions to the arts in Idaho are recognized, particularly through Boise Music Week's tradition of supporting the arts. Kay notes her mother's love for the arts and her influence as a teacher, reinforcing the family's commitment to cultural enrichment.
# Mother’s Influence and Father’s Death

> Kay describes her mother's passion for the arts and her career as a teacher in Nampa, particularly at Kenwood Grade School. She shares the tragic story of her father's early death from untreated high blood pressure and reflects on how medical advances might have prevented it. After her father's passing, her mother returned to teaching, demonstrating resilience and dedication to her family.
# Teaching Ballet for Pat Harris

> Kay recounts her long-standing relationship with Pat Harris, whom she knew from high school and dance classes with Mrs. Van De Steeg. She discusses her years teaching ballet for Pat Harris, the positive impact on generations of students, and the supportive environment that allowed her to focus on teaching while raising her own children. Kay acknowledges Pat Harris's significant role in fostering dance education in the community.
# Dance Community and Lloyd Carlton

> The conversation expands to the broader dance community, mentioning Lloyd Carlton, his studio in downtown Boise, and collaborative activities among dance studios. Kay recalls teaching for Lloyd and the shared experiences of dance teachers, including joint performances and the Boise Valley Dance Teachers Association, which promoted unity and professional development among local instructors.
# Dance Camps and Teacher Networks

> Kay and the interviewer reminisce about dance camps in Twin Falls and Sun Valley, as well as the collaborative spirit among Idaho dance teachers. They discuss the energizing effect of these camps and the importance of teacher networks in fostering creativity and professional growth. The chapter highlights the sense of community and shared purpose among dance educators.
# Grandparents and Heritage

> Kay shares memories of her grandmother Nora Morgan Whissel and her family's heritage, including Irish, German, and English roots. She recalls visiting relatives in Illinois and describes her grandparents' physical characteristics, noting the generational changes in height. The discussion provides insight into Kay's ancestry and the diverse cultural influences in her family.
# Mrs. Van De Steeg and Dance Teaching Traditions

> Kay talks about her dance teacher Mrs. Van De Steeg, who settled in Nampa after marrying a local attorney. She describes Mrs. Van De Steeg's background in traveling performance, her distinctive teaching style using a stick and wearing heels, and the traditional methods of dance instruction in that era. Kay contrasts these practices with her own approach to teaching dance.
# Closing Reflections

> The interview concludes with Kay expressing satisfaction with the topics covered and appreciation for the opportunity to document her life story. The interviewer thanks Kay for her time and contributions to dance in the Treasure Valley, offering to record additional sessions if Kay wishes to add more memories in the future. The conversation ends on a warm and grateful note.

story.title: Call with Kaye Andrijeski
story.description: Phone interview with Kaye Andrijeski on September 5, 2025

participants.name: Kaye Mitchell Andrijeski and Aleta Sales Pottenger
